brief: GAP/RATIONALE: Refractory ventricular tachycardia (VT) persists despite maximal drug + extensive catheter ablation in high-risk patients; cardiac stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) is an emerging noninvasive option; safety/effectiveness data, especially at 1 year, are limited. DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective single-arm registry; patients enrolled after failing maximal medical and ablation therapy. INTERVENTION: Cardiac SBRT; plan target volume built by integrating available electrophysiologic mapping + imaging data; single 25 Gy fraction; respiratory motion mitigation strategies used. COMPARISON: Each patient served as own control — outcomes compared with the 6 months before treatment. POPULATION/N: 15 patients enrolled and underwent planning; 14 (93%) actually received treatment. ENDPOINTS: VT burden = ICD shocks + antitachycardia pacing (ATP) sequences; plus clinical and safety outcomes; assessed at 6 weeks, 6 months, 12 months. KEY RESULTS: survival 12/15 i.e. 12 (80%) survived to end of 6-week period; 10 (67%) survived to 12 months. VT recurrence (appropriate ATP or ICD shocks) from 6 weeks to 12 months in 33% (4 of 12). Significant reduction in treated VT: 98% reduction at 6 weeks to 6 months; 99% reduction at 12 months, vs the 6 months pre-treatment. Nonsignificant trend toward lower amiodarone dose at 12 months. 4 deaths occurred after treatment. No changes in ventricular function. TAKEAWAY: In a select high-risk group with VT refractory to standard therapy, SBRT is associated with reduced VT and reduced appropriate ICD therapies over 1 year.
